fr:mainmatter.html:p: Brief notes on Variational Inference with Normalizing Flows to motivate flow matching.

We are concerned with the problem of density estimation. Given a dataset \mathcal {X}, consisting of samples \mathbf {x}_i \sim p(\mathbf {x}), we want to learn p(\mathbf {x}). One popular way to do this is via variational inference. As a brief recap, in variational inference, we assume that there is actually a joint distribution p(\mathbf {x}, \mathbf {z}), where \mathbf {z} is a latent variable. We then find p(\mathbf {x}) by maximizing the log-likelihood \log \int p(\mathbf {x} \mid \mathbf {z}) \mathrm {d}\mathbf {z}

For an example of this, see my post on expectation maximization. In that post, we utilize the ELBO to learn a latent variable model q_{\phi }(\mathbf {z} | \mathbf {x}). As discussed in my post on ELBO, in order to learn p(\mathbf {x}), we must have that p(\mathbf {z} | \mathbf {x}) is in the family q_{\phi }. However, q_ {\phi } is usually a Gaussian parametrized by \phi , so that we can easily learn \phi . In this case, it is very unlikely that p(\mathbf {z} \mid x) lies in the family of q_{\phi } and therefore we have no hope of learning the true p(\mathbf {x}).

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Variational Inference with Normalizing Flows

fr:title.text: Flow Matching: What, Why, and How?

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Flow Matching: What, Why, and How?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This is a blog cataloging my understanding of Flow Matching for Generative Modeling prior to my group presentation on Temporal Difference Flows.
Disclaimer. I came into this with literally no background. I have never even taken an ODE course. This may prove advantageous for the more general audience.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
To understand flow matching, it is a useful endeavor to understand the problems in prior work it aims to solve. 2025715https://kellenkanarios.com/B9J9/B9J9/B9J9/Normalizing Flows
Brief notes on Variational Inference with Normalizing Flows to motivate flow matching.
We are concerned with the problem of density estimation. Given a dataset \mathcal {X}, consisting of samples \mathbf {x}_i \sim p(\mathbf {x}), we want to learn p(\mathbf {x}). One popular way to do this is via variational inference. As a brief recap, in variational inference, we assume that there is actually a joint distribution p(\mathbf {x}, \mathbf {z}), where \mathbf {z} is a latent variable. We then find p(\mathbf {x}) by maximizing the log-likelihood \log \int p(\mathbf {x} \mid \mathbf {z}) \mathrm {d}\mathbf {z}
For an example of this, see my post on expectation maximization. In that post, we utilize the ELBO to learn a latent variable model q_{\phi }(\mathbf {z} | \mathbf {x}). As discussed in my post on ELBO, in order to learn p(\mathbf {x}), we must have that p(\mathbf {z} | \mathbf {x}) is in the family q_{\phi }. However, q_ {\phi } is usually a Gaussian parametrized by \phi , so that we can easily learn \phi . In this case, it is very unlikely that p(\mathbf {z} \mid x) lies in the family of q_{\phi } and therefore we have no hope of learning the true p(\mathbf {x}).
Kellen Kanarioshttps://kellenkanarios.com/PY9P/PY9P/PY9P/Continuous Normalizing Flows
Variational Inference with Normalizing Flows, Neural Ordinary Differential Equations Kellen KanariosWhat is this \det factor? Since we are considering probability measures over \mathbb {R}^n, it is not enough to just define a mapping between spaces. Explicitly, say that we are considering the two probability measures p \sim \mathrm {Unif}[0,1] and q \sim \mathrm {Unif}[0,2]. Trivially, we have the mapping f : [0, 1] \to [0,2] given by f(x) \mapsto 2x.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The probability density path p: [0,1] \times \mathbb {R}^d \to \mathbb {R}_{>0} is a time dependent probability density function i.e. \int p_t(x)\mathrm {d}x = 1
We define a time-dependent vector field as a map v: [0,1] \times \mathbb {R}^d \to \mathbb {R}^d

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Modeling a vector field via a neural network i.e. v_t(x; \theta ) implicitly defines a deep parametric model of the flow \phi _t(x) via solving the ODE or an approximation, such as \phi _{t + \Delta t}(x) \approx \phi _t(x) + \Delta t \cdot v_t(\phi _t(x); \theta ) Obviously, accuracy will be dependent on the step size \Delta t.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We say that a vector field generates a probability density path if the flow of v_t satisfies p_t = [\phi _t]_* p_{0}, where [\phi _t]_*p_{0}(x) = p_{0}(\phi _t^{-1}(x)) \det \left [ \frac {\partial \phi _t^{-1} }{\partial x}(x) \right ].
A vector field generates a probability density path if it satisfies the continuity equation.

Given an initial noise distribution p, a desired data distribution q, a probability density path p_t, such that p_{0} = p and p_{1} =q, and a vector field u_t that generates p_t. We define the flow matching loss \mathcal {L}_{\mathrm {FM}} as \mathcal {L}_{\mathrm {FM}}(\theta ) \coloneqq \mathbb {E}_{t, p_t(x)} \|v_t(x; \theta ) - u_t\|

Given data distribution q(x), we define an approximation p(x) with the form p(x) = \int p(x \mid x_1) q(x_1) \mathrm {d}x_{1} In order to ensure p(x) \approx q(x), we pick p(x \mid x_{1}) that concentrates around x_{1} i.e. p(x \mid x_{1}) \sim N(x \mid x_{1}, \sigma ^2 I) for small \sigma .
We define the conditional vector field v_t(\cdot \mid x_{1}) as the vector field that generates the conditional probability path p_t(x \mid x_{1}).
Given a conditional vector field v_t(x \mid x_{1}) we define the marginal vector field as v_t(x) = \int v_t(x \mid x_1) \frac {p(x \mid x_1) q(x_{1})}{p(t)}

Given a conditional vector field v_t(x \mid x_{1}) that generates the conditional probability path p_t(x \mid x_{1}) then the marginal vector field v_t generates p_t, where p_t(x) = \int p_t(x \mid x_1) q(x_{1})
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Proof. This follows from just plugging into continuity equation.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: which is supposedly a "good" approximation of the data distribution q. We can then show that a conditional flow matching loss has the same gradient as the full flow matching loss i.e. our vector field should converge to the marginal vector field, which is a good approximation for q.
To generate samples, we integrate the vector field
Important. We do not have the flow. We only have the flow for a given x_{1}. This can then provide a target for the vector field with equivalent gradient.
Notes and thoughts from blog Diffusion Meets Flow Matching: Two Sides of the Same Coin. As a useful pre-requisite, I recommend my notes on diffusion and flow matching respectively.
To start, I began looking into this due to the wildly empirical claims from Flow Matching for Generative Modeling. Namely,

html:li.html:em: "We find that using FM provides more robust and stable training, and achieves superior performance compared to score matching."
"Furthermore, we find that our models produce better trade-offs between computational cost and sample quality."

fr:title.text: Flow Matching: What, Why, and How?

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Flow Matching: What, Why, and How?

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This is a blog cataloging my understanding of Flow Matching for Generative Modeling prior to my group presentation on Temporal Difference Flows.
Disclaimer. I came into this with literally no background. I have never even taken an ODE course. This may prove advantageous for the more general audience.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter:
To understand flow matching, it is a useful endeavor to understand the problems in prior work it aims to solve. 2025715https://kellenkanarios.com/B9J9/B9J9/B9J9/Normalizing Flows
Brief notes on Variational Inference with Normalizing Flows to motivate flow matching.
We are concerned with the problem of density estimation. Given a dataset \mathcal {X}, consisting of samples \mathbf {x}_i \sim p(\mathbf {x}), we want to learn p(\mathbf {x}). One popular way to do this is via variational inference. As a brief recap, in variational inference, we assume that there is actually a joint distribution p(\mathbf {x}, \mathbf {z}), where \mathbf {z} is a latent variable. We then find p(\mathbf {x}) by maximizing the log-likelihood \log \int p(\mathbf {x} \mid \mathbf {z}) \mathrm {d}\mathbf {z}
For an example of this, see my post on expectation maximization. In that post, we utilize the ELBO to learn a latent variable model q_{\phi }(\mathbf {z} | \mathbf {x}). As discussed in my post on ELBO, in order to learn p(\mathbf {x}), we must have that p(\mathbf {z} | \mathbf {x}) is in the family q_{\phi }. However, q_ {\phi } is usually a Gaussian parametrized by \phi , so that we can easily learn \phi . In this case, it is very unlikely that p(\mathbf {z} \mid x) lies in the family of q_{\phi } and therefore we have no hope of learning the true p(\mathbf {x}).
Kellen Kanarioshttps://kellenkanarios.com/PY9P/PY9P/PY9P/Continuous Normalizing Flows
Variational Inference with Normalizing Flows, Neural Ordinary Differential Equations Kellen KanariosWhat is this \det factor? Since we are considering probability measures over \mathbb {R}^n, it is not enough to just define a mapping between spaces. Explicitly, say that we are considering the two probability measures p \sim \mathrm {Unif}[0,1] and q \sim \mathrm {Unif}[0,2]. Trivially, we have the mapping f : [0, 1] \to [0,2] given by f(x) \mapsto 2x.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The probability density path p: [0,1] \times \mathbb {R}^d \to \mathbb {R}_{>0} is a time dependent probability density function i.e. \int p_t(x)\mathrm {d}x = 1
We define a time-dependent vector field as a map v: [0,1] \times \mathbb {R}^d \to \mathbb {R}^d

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Modeling a vector field via a neural network i.e. v_t(x; \theta ) implicitly defines a deep parametric model of the flow \phi _t(x) via solving the ODE or an approximation, such as \phi _{t + \Delta t}(x) \approx \phi _t(x) + \Delta t \cdot v_t(\phi _t(x); \theta ) Obviously, accuracy will be dependent on the step size \Delta t.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We say that a vector field generates a probability density path if the flow of v_t satisfies p_t = [\phi _t]_* p_{0}, where [\phi _t]_*p_{0}(x) = p_{0}(\phi _t^{-1}(x)) \det \left [ \frac {\partial \phi _t^{-1} }{\partial x}(x) \right ].
A vector field generates a probability density path if it satisfies the continuity equation.

Given an initial noise distribution p, a desired data distribution q, a probability density path p_t, such that p_{0} = p and p_{1} =q, and a vector field u_t that generates p_t. We define the flow matching loss \mathcal {L}_{\mathrm {FM}} as \mathcal {L}_{\mathrm {FM}}(\theta ) \coloneqq \mathbb {E}_{t, p_t(x)} \|v_t(x; \theta ) - u_t\|

Given data distribution q(x), we define an approximation p(x) with the form p(x) = \int p(x \mid x_1) q(x_1) \mathrm {d}x_{1} In order to ensure p(x) \approx q(x), we pick p(x \mid x_{1}) that concentrates around x_{1} i.e. p(x \mid x_{1}) \sim N(x \mid x_{1}, \sigma ^2 I) for small \sigma .
We define the conditional vector field v_t(\cdot \mid x_{1}) as the vector field that generates the conditional probability path p_t(x \mid x_{1}).
Given a conditional vector field v_t(x \mid x_{1}) we define the marginal vector field as v_t(x) = \int v_t(x \mid x_1) \frac {p(x \mid x_1) q(x_{1})}{p(t)}

Given a conditional vector field v_t(x \mid x_{1}) that generates the conditional probability path p_t(x \mid x_{1}) then the marginal vector field v_t generates p_t, where p_t(x) = \int p_t(x \mid x_1) q(x_{1})
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Proof. This follows from just plugging into continuity equation.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: which is supposedly a "good" approximation of the data distribution q. We can then show that a conditional flow matching loss has the same gradient as the full flow matching loss i.e. our vector field should converge to the marginal vector field, which is a good approximation for q.
To generate samples, we integrate the vector field
Important. We do not have the flow. We only have the flow for a given x_{1}. This can then provide a target for the vector field with equivalent gradient.
Notes and thoughts from blog Diffusion Meets Flow Matching: Two Sides of the Same Coin. As a useful pre-requisite, I recommend my notes on diffusion and flow matching respectively.
To start, I began looking into this due to the wildly empirical claims from Flow Matching for Generative Modeling. Namely,

html:li.html:em: "We find that using FM provides more robust and stable training, and achieves superior performance compared to score matching."
"Furthermore, we find that our models produce better trade-offs between computational cost and sample quality."

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Remark. Note that the gradient is with respect to \theta , where \theta is just the third argument to \mathsf {ELBO} as defined above. Notably, \theta _t will show up elsewhere later but will NOT be backpropagated through.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The above motivates the expectation step of the EM algorithm. Namely how can we compute the lower bound expectation for the best possible q.

Recall (or review) from the discussion on ELBO that q(z) = p(z | x; \theta ) maximizes the ELBO for a given x and \theta . Therefore, we actually want to learn q_{\phi }(z, x, \theta ) \approx p(z | x; \theta ). To do so, we can simply maximize the ELBO (duh). Formally, \phi _{t+1} = \argmax _{\phi } \mathbb {E}_{x \sim \mathcal {D}, z \sim q}\left [\log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta _t)}{q_{\phi }(Z, x, \theta _t)}\right ]
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Question. Now this is all cool theoretically but how TF do we actually maximize this thing?
Answer. It is highly dependent on the parametrization of p and q.

html:div.html:p:
We define the problem as
p(z) \sim N(0, 1)
p(x | z; \theta ) \sim N(\mu _{\theta }(z), \sigma _{\theta }(z))
q_{\phi }(z | x, \theta ) \sim N(\mu _{\phi }(x), \sigma _\phi (x)) where \mu _{\{\phi , \theta \}}, \sigma _{\{\phi , \theta \}} are parametrized neural networks. Then we can rewrite the ELBO as (ignoring constants)
\begin {align*} \mathsf {ELBO}(x, q, \theta ) &= \mathbb {E}_{x \sim \mathcal {D}, z \sim q(\cdot \mid x, \theta )} \left [\log p(Z) - \log \frac {p(x \mid Z; \theta )}{q_{\phi }(Z, x, \theta )}\right ] \\ &= \mathbb {E}_{x \sim \mathcal {D}, z \sim q(\cdot \mid x, \theta )} \left [-\frac {Z^2}{2} - \frac {\sigma _{\phi }(x)^2}{\sigma _{\theta }(z)^2}\big ((Z - \mu _{\theta }(Z))^2 - (Z - \mu _{\phi }(x))^2\big )\right ] .\end {align*}

fr:mainmatter.html:div: Problem. We need to sample realization z \sim q_{\phi }(\cdot |x, \theta ). So isn't our realization z a function of \phi . How would we optimize this?
fr:mainmatter.html:p: Now for a given sample x_i \sim \mathcal {D}, we can get a sample z_i(\phi ) \sim q(\cdot \mid x_i, \theta ) that is differentiable with respect to \phi . Using this, we can get the stochastic gradient estimate \nabla _q \widetilde {\mathsf {ELBO}}(x, q, \theta ) = \nabla _{\phi } \left [\frac {z_i(\phi )^2}{2} - \frac {\sigma _{\phi }(x)^2}{\sigma _{\theta }(z)^2}\big ((z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\theta }(z_i(\phi )))^2 - (z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\phi }(x))^2\big )\right ]

Finally, we have reached the maximization step. This step is fairly straightforward after all the progress we have made. Once we have a tight lower bound i.e. q(z) \approx p(z \mid x; \theta ) then we simply fix q and maximize the ELBO for \theta i.e. in the above example and algorithm we instead compute \nabla _\theta \widetilde {\mathsf {ELBO}}(x, q, \theta ) = \nabla _{\theta } \left [\frac {z_i(\phi )^2}{2} - \frac {\sigma _{\phi }(x)^2}{\sigma _{\theta }(z)^2}\big ((z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\theta }(z_i(\phi )))^2 - (z_i(\phi ) - \mu _{\phi }(x))^2\big )\right ] Alternating between these steps yields the expectation-maximization algorithm the ML oldheads mythologize.
Given a joint distribution p(X,Z) for two random variables X and Z. We call a random variable a latent random variable if we have a dataset \mathcal {D} that only consists of realizations of X.

Setup. We are given a joint distribution p(X, Z; \theta ), where X and Z are random variables with their joint parametrized by \theta . We are also given a dataset \mathcal {D} containing realizations of only X i.e. Z is a latent random variable.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Goal. We want to learn p(x; \theta ).
fr:mainmatter.html:p: However, p(x; \theta ) = \int p(x, z; \theta ) \mathrm {d}z = \int p(x | z; \theta )p(z) \mathrm {d}z, which is computationally intractable for most continuous Z.
fr:mainmatter.html:div: Question. Can we update \theta for a given realization of x and z?
fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Motivation. Suppose we had some q(z) that we could sample realizations of Z from. Then we want to find some reasonable \ell _{\theta }, such that \theta ^* \approx \arg \max _{\theta } \mathbb {E}_{x, \sim p(x; \theta ^*), z \sim q(z)}[\ell _{\theta }(X, Z)]
Remark. q(z) can potentially (and will) depend on the sample x_i. This is OK because we are given x_i's, which we can then just pass to q to get the corresponding z_i.
Why is this the case? Because then (for well-behaved \ell _{\theta }) \theta _{t + 1} = \theta _{t} - \alpha \nabla _{\theta _{t}}\ell _{\theta _{t}}(x_i, z_i) \to \theta ^* i.e. SGD is a reasonable thing to do, where the gradient is for a single sample of x_i and z_i which we have readily available from our dataset \mathcal {D} and our distribution q that we can sample from. This idea comes up a lot in optimization, such as the \log trick in the original policy gradient.

Similar to the \log trick, the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) comes from a simple multiply and divide trick. Namely, \begin {align*} \log p(x; \theta ) &= \log \int p(x, z; \theta ) \mathrm {d}z \\ &= \log \int p(x, z; \theta ) \frac {q(z)}{q(z)} \mathrm {d}z \\ &= \log \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} \\ &\geq \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} .\end {align*} The last inequality follows directly from Jensen's Inequality. However, note that this is only a lower bound and therefore not exactly what we want. For example, if we were to pick q \sim N(0, 1) and then optimize the ELBO for \theta we would not necessarily find a good \theta for the likelihood \log p(x; \theta ). Luckily, we can actually get the difference \log p(x; \theta ) - ELBO in closed form! Namely, \begin {align*} \log p(x; \theta ) - \mathrm {ELBO} &= \log p(x; \theta ) - \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} \\ &= \int \left (\log p(x; \theta ) - \log \frac {p(x, z; \theta )}{q(z)}\right )q(z) \\ &= \int \left (\log \frac {p(x; \theta )q(z)}{p(x, z; \theta )}\right )q(z) \\ &= \int \left (\log \frac {p(x; \theta )q(z)}{p(z | x; \theta )p(x; \theta )}\right )q(z) \\ &= \int \left (\log \frac {q(z)}{p(z | x; \theta )}\right )q(z) \\ &= \mathrm {KL}\big (q(z) \mid \mid p(z | x; \theta )\big ) .\end {align*} Thus, to tighten the lower bound we can optimize q_{\phi }(z) to try to match p(z | x; \theta ). As a sanity check, lets make sure these match when q(z) = p(z \mid x; \theta ). \begin {align*} \mathbb {E}_{z \sim q(z)} \log \frac {p(x, Z; \theta )}{q(z)} &= \int \log \frac {p(x, z; \theta )}{q(z)}\mathrm {d}z \\ &= \int \log \frac {p(x; \theta )p(z \mid x; \theta )}{p(z \mid x; \theta )}\mathrm {d}z \\ &= \int \log p(x; \theta )\mathrm {d}z \\ &= \log (x; \theta ) .\end {align*} You may be wondering how we use this algorithmically to find \theta ^*? This will be the main idea of expectation maximization. Feel free to check it out!
